phool dei फूलदेई 
## in two lines

on the first morning of chaitra, kumaon's children — the **phoolyari** — go door to door laying fyunli and buransh petals on freshly cleaned thresholds, singing *फूलदेई, छम्मा देई, दैणी द्वार, भर भकार* — "flowers on the doorstep, may the grain-store fill" [v — chant corroborated verbatim across three outlets]. the house answers with rice, jaggery, and the day's sweet, **sei** [a].

## what it is

**the rite.** housewives plaster the threshold with cow-dung/clay wash the evening before; at dawn children arrive with baskets of flowers, rice, jaggery and coconut, scatter petals on the deli (threshold), sing the blessing, and are rewarded [v — down to earth + hindi wikipedia + etv independently]. the chant's sense: *may this door be auspicious, this house free of conflict, this bhakar (grain-store) full; to this threshold, greetings again and again* [v].

**the flowers.** **fyunli/pheonly** (*reinwardtia indica* — the little golden flax of spring cliffsides) and **buransh** (rhododendron, the state tree) are the two verified core flowers; the wider list (mustard, peach, apricot, semal) is attested for the garhwal telling [v/a]. a separate "payoli = wild cherry" species claim did not survive verification — fyunli is one flower, not a cherry [v — negative].

**the sweet.** **sei/sai (शाइ)** — soaked-rice flour, jaggery, ghee — is the festival dish, but rests on a single source (hindi wikipedia); flagged accordingly, and exactly the kind of thing one phone call to a grandmother resolves [u — gap kept open]. **ghughuti sweets do not belong to phool dei** — re-verified across every source: the crow-garland belongs to uttarayani in january; any conflation is an error this archive rejects [v — negative].

**duration & the present.** kumaon: one day (chaitra 1); garhwal: up to the whole month with a closing ghogha mata rite — a real regional split, recorded as such [v]. the festival now has state-level embrace: the cm has celebrated it with children annually (2023–2026, three wire services), framing it as a children's festival — adoption the assimilation metric can only envy [v].

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| फूलदेई | phūldeī | the festival; "flowers to the threshold" |
| फूल्यारी | phūlyārī | the flower-carrying children |
| देली | delī | the threshold that receives the petals |
| भकार | bhakār | the grain-store the chant blesses |
| फ्यूंली | phyūnlī | reinwardtia indica — spring's yellow herald |
| शाइ / सै | śāi / sai | the rice-jaggery festival sweet |

## do this at home (14 march 2027)

- **let the children do it** — even a diaspora balcony has a threshold: any yellow flowers, the four-line chant (learn it from the entry; it's eight seconds), and a sweet handed over. this is the most portable festival kumaon owns.
- **make sei** — soaked rice ground with jaggery and a spoon of ghee — and tell us how your family's version differs; that report closes an open gap.
- **teach the word bhakar** and what it held.

the ritual landscape of kumaon रीति-रिवाज 
## in two lines

kumaon's ritual world runs from the courtyard to the courtroom of the gods: weddings sealed by interlocking yellow cloth (**aanchal bandhan**), a justice-god at chitai who accepts **written petitions** and repays verdicts in brass bells, and the **jagar** night-vigils in which deities speak through a village medium [v/a]. this entry maps that landscape; the deepest rooms in it — jagar above all — are living religious practice, documented here with respect and without spectacle.

## what it is

**life-cycle rites.** naming on the 11th day with the infant's first sun (**surya darshan**), on an aipan chowki [a]. the wedding sequence is kumaon's richest documented rite: **suwal pathai** (women of both houses prepare wheat-dough suwal days ahead, with black-sesame effigies of the in-laws exchanged in play) [a — well-sourced with named informants]; ganesh puja and the lighting of the **pradhaan diya**; **dhuliarghya** — the bride's father washing the groom's feet in the courtyard; kanyadaan traditionally in the **goth**, the house's ground floor; and **aanchal bandhan**, the interlocking of the groom's and bride's yellow cloth, which — not sindoor alone — makes the marriage [a]. the bride wears the rangwali pichhoda; her first act at her new village is prayer at its naula [v]. death rites close with the dotless aipan of the 12th day, wiped away when mourning ends [v]. **honesty notes**: "pahal" as a named first-feeding rite and kumaon-specific janeu procedure could not be verified [u]; and **karva chauth is documented as *not* traditional to kumaon** — a recent urban adoption [v].

**the gods of the land.** **golu devta**, god of justice, incarnation of gaur bhairav — his chitai darbar hung with thousands of **written petitions** (stamp-paper appeals among them, in a genuinely court-like idiom: devotees call chitai the supreme court, ghorakhal the high court) and the bells of answered prayers [v — academic fieldwork]. his origin legend is told in variants (a silbatta or a pumpkin swapped for the infant prince) — variants recorded, none crowned [a]. around him a full pantheon: **gangnath** (the doti prince of the tragic romance), **haru and saim** (paired temples), **bholanath**, **nanda devi** — whose kumaoni september melas at almora (temple built by the chand kings), nainital and bageshwar are distinct from garhwal's twelve-yearly raj jat [v]; **airy**, the fierce forest-pastoral deity [v — two source families]; **chaumu** the cattle-protector of the pancheswar belt [a — single chain]; and **bhumiya**, the land-guardian of nearly every village, first-invoked by the hurkiya before sowing [a — cross-corroborated].

**jagar.** the awakening: a **jagariya** bard sings deity legends to hurka and thali through the night; the deity descends into the **dangariya** medium; the household's questions are answered [v]. tradition distinguishes **dev jagar** from **bhoot/pitru jagar** [a]. local taxonomy separates benevolent **rajanaya** ancestor-deities (offered puri-halwa) from **preth** deities of violent death (offered differently; women traditionally excluded from some airy sessions) [a — academic fieldwork, single source]. one correction the record demands: the celebrated padma shri jagar singer **basanti bisht is garhwali (chamoli)** — honored here as a neighbor, not claimed [v]. this node's full entry awaits the charter's sensitivity review; no recordings are embedded.

**the turning year** (beyond harela, olgia, ghughutia — documented separately): **khatarua** (ashwin 1, ~mid-september) — evening bonfires, cucumbers thrown to the flames, cattle-sheds cleaned: an autumn cattle-and-hearth rite [v — incl. print source]. its popular origin legend — a chand victory over garhwal — is **actively disputed**, with a homelier etymology (airing the monsoon-damp *khatad* quilts) equally attested; both recorded, neither crowned [a — dispute recorded]. and the spelling "dhatarua" appears in no source — the festival is khatarua/khatadua [v — negative]. **phool dei** (chaitra 1): children — *phoolyaris* — lay burans, fyunli and painya petals on thresholds singing *phool dei, chamma dei*, and are given rice, jaggery and **sei**; its full entry ships before march 2027 on the festival clock [v]. **ganga dussehra dwar-patra** — mantra-inscribed door papers on nearly every kumaoni door [a]. bat savitri is observed but not distinctively kumaoni in any found source [a — generic].

## do this at home

- **at the next family wedding**, watch for the aanchal bandhan and the dhuliarghya — then ask the oldest woman present what suwal pathai was like in her village. write it down.
- **visit chitai** when in almora: read the petitions (respectfully), and ask an elder whether the family ever wrote one.
- **this september (khatarua, ~17 sept 2026 — verify locally)**: tell both origin stories — the disputed battle and the quilts — and let the family argue; the argument is the tradition.
- **diaspora**: on ganga dussehra, print or hand-draw a dwar-patra for your door.

the agricultural year खेती-बाड़ी क साल 
## in two lines

the kumaoni field-year runs on three seasons — **rudi** (summer), **chaumas** (the monsoon that feeds the kharif), **hyund** (the winter of wheat and barley) — compressed in the proverb *ह्यूंद हिमाल, चौमास माल* [a/v]. its grains are the identity crops: **madua** (finger millet) and **maadira** (barnyard millet — the kumaoni name; "jhangora" is garhwali), bhatt, gahat — sown late may to june by the agricultural university's own advisory, exactly as grandmothers' rules say [v].

## what it is

**the calendar.** native ethnography gives three seasons (rudi/chaumas/hyund); the extension-science framing adds jayad as a third cropping window — a richer local model that popular "kharif/rabi" talk compresses [v/a — both recorded]. sowing windows from gbpuat's official advisory: madua and barnyard millet in the second fortnight of may to mid-june (altitude-dependent), amaranth and buckwheat likewise, gahat in early june [v — t1]. harela's sowing rite sits deliberately at the monsoon hinge of this calendar (see that entry).

**the system.** kumaon's mixed cropping and biennial terrace rotation is documented academically as the **sari/saar system** [v — springer]. **"baranaja" is a garhwali term** — coined around tehri's beej bachao andolan (vijay jardhari) — and is *not* the native kumaoni word; even kumaon-based sources credit it to garhwal. recorded and corrected [v — three sources]. irrigation runs on **guls** — gravity-fed contour channels (diversion dam *baan*; irrigating *kulayana*; branch channels *hawarr*, *baul*) that also drove the **gharat** watermills [v — t1 via ijtk/cpreec].

**the labor, honestly.** threshing in the courtyard under oxen with work-songs to the bullocks and bhumiya [a — single rich ethnographic essay]. and the load-bearing fact of the whole system: women carry it — a bhimtal-block study (n=260) records 12–16-hour days and 75% with lower-back pain; male out-migration has made women "the backbone of the hill economy" [v — t1 + t2]. (a frequently cited hill-women time-use study is from assam, not kumaon — excluded [v — negative].)

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| रुड़ी · चौमास · ह्यूंद | ruṛī · chaumās · hyūnd | summer · monsoon · winter — the three seasons |
| मडुवा · मादिरा | maḍuvā · mādirā | finger millet · barnyard millet (kumaoni name) |
| सार / सारी | sār / sārī | the rotating terrace-block cropping system |
| गूल · बाण | gūl · bāṇ | irrigation channel · its diversion dam |
| घराट | gharāṭ | the water-mill the gul drives |
| खलिहान | khalihān | threshing floor (generic term; no distinct kumaoni custom verified) |

## do this at home

- **cook the calendar**: madua roti in hyund, bhatt in winter dals — eating seasonally *is* the agricultural literacy.
- **ask what your family's sari grew** — which terraces, which rotation, whose oxen; one conversation captures a system the web barely knows.
- **find the gharat**: ask if the village mill still turns; photograph it either way.

the forest: flora & fauna of kumaon बण-जंगल 
## in two lines

kumaon's culture is written in two trees: the broadleaf **banj** oak that holds the water and feeds the cattle, and the colonial-favored **chir** pine that spread with the railways — a quarrel that burned through the 1916–21 forest protests and produced the world-notable **van panchayat** village forest councils of 1931 [v]. and it is sung in species: the *kafal pako* cuckoo, the ghughuti dove of the migration songs, the burans that opens spring [v/a].

## what it is

**oak versus pine.** banj oak (c. 1500–2300 m) anchors water tables and fodder; chir pine (c. 900–1800 m) spread massively under colonial forestry for resin and railway sleepers from the 1860s onward [v]. resentment of reserved forests ignited the **1916–1921 kumaon agitation** — chir plantations burned across almora — forcing the kumaon forest grievances committee (1921) and then the **van panchayat rules of 1931**: community-managed village forests, a system still running today with counts reported between ~11,200 and ~12,167 (the uttarakhand economic survey figure; range recorded, not resolved) [v/a]. **chipko honesty**: the famous 1973–74 tree-hugging is garhwal's story (mandal, reni, gaura devi); kumaon's forest politics ran through its own earlier protests and later merged into the statehood movement [v — attribution kept straight].

**trees of the culture** (each verified): **kafal** (*myrica esculenta*) — the summer fruit whose folklore says a wrongly beaten daughter became the bird crying *kafal pako, mil ni chakho*; the caller is the indian cuckoo per ethno-ornithology, though one telling paints a magpie — conflict recorded [a]. **burans** (*rhododendron arboreum*), the state tree, its petals gathered by children for **phool dei** [v]. **deodar**, the "wood of the gods," ringing jageshwar's shrines [v — grove; u — purana specifics]. **bhimal** for fodder and rope-fibre [v]; **ringal** for gi-tagged basketry [v]; **timur** (*zanthoxylum armatum*), collected and traded by the bhotiya of pithoragarh [v — peer-reviewed]; **utis** alder pollarded on terraces [v — icraf]; **hisalu**, the golden raspberry praised by the poet gumaani pant [a]; **kilmora**, **ghingaru** [a/u]; **pinalu** taro, whose prized *gaderi* comes from lobanj, bageshwar [v — peer-reviewed].

**animals of the story.** the **champawat tigress** — a tiger, ~436 deaths, shot by jim corbett in 1907 — and the **panar leopard** (almora, ~400 deaths, 1910) are kumaon's man-eaters; the more famous rudraprayag leopard is **garhwal's**, a standard conflation this archive refuses [v]. leopard conflict is present-tense: ~234 deaths statewide 2011–2021, with behaviour-change programs measurably reducing attacks [v]. the **ghughuti** dove (*streptopelia*) carries the ache of the married daughter's longing in kumaon's best-loved songs [v — peer-reviewed folklore study]; the **monal** is the state bird [v]; monkey crop-raiding is now itself a driver of field abandonment [v].

**protected geography.** corbett np (1936, first national park of mainland asia) [v]; binsar wls (1988, created for the oak) [v]; askot musk deer sanctuary (1986, pithoragarh) [v]; nanda devi biosphere reserve — shared, majority garhwal [v — flagged shared].

## do this at home

- **learn the two trees**: on any hill drive, teach a child to tell banj from chir — and why the difference decided a century of protest.
- **taste the calendar**: kafal in may–june, hisalu after it, burans juice in spring — ask which the family picked as children, and where.
- **harela 2027**: plant the sapling where it can live; the festival's modern plantation drives are the culture's own conservation instrument.
- **retell one story**: the kafal-pako girl, or the champawat tigress — both are kumaon's own.

sacred geography & pilgrimage देवभूमि 
## in two lines

kumaon's sacred map is a working geography: jageshwar's deodar temple-city, bageshwar at the saryu-gomati confluence, purnagiri drawing millions above the kali, the cave of patal bhuvaneshwar, and the kailash-mansarovar road climbing to lipulekh [v]. the state is now wiring these together as the **manaskhand mandir mala mission**, a kumaon answer to the char dham circuit [v — rti-confirmed].

## what it is

**the anchor sites.** **jageshwar** — ~125 temples in deodar, 7th–8th c.; its claim to be the nageshwar jyotirlinga is genuinely contested (dwarka and aundha claim it too), so it's recorded as a claim resting on the puranic "darukavan" identification, not a settled fact [a/v — contested, recorded]. **bageshwar/baghnath** at the saryu-gomati sangam, in the skanda purana, with a katyuri-era inscription; present structure 1450 or 1602 (sources split) [v/a]. **purnagiri** above tanakpur — a shakti peeth of the sati cycle (the "108 siddha peeth" framing is inconsistent across sources), drawing 2.2 million in a reported year, under a 2024 state renovation [v/a]. **patal bhuvaneshwar**, the gangolihat limestone cave — its "skanda purana manaskhand 103rd chapter" citation is a tourism copy-chain, not text-verified [a — attributed]; discovery-by-ritupurna, rediscovery-by-shankaracharya are legend [u]. **haat kalika** (gangolihat), patron goddess of the kumaon regiment ("kalika mata ki jai") — the installation legend splits between 1971 and wwii across sources [v for the regiment link; u for the date].

**kasar devi, carefully.** vivekananda meditated near it (sept 1890, per the district administration) [v]; crank's ridge drew beat poets and govinda in the 1960s [v]; the "van allen belt / cosmic energy" claim is **unsubstantiated pseudoscience** — no nasa or peer-reviewed source supports it, and even tourism sites concede it "lacks verification." recorded as legend, not fact [v — negative].

**the living pilgrimage.** the **kailash-mansarovar** route through kumaon to **lipulekh** — its 80-km bro road completed may 2020, amid an active india-nepal boundary dispute (1816 sugauli treaty, kali-source question) [v]. and **nanda devi** as sacred peak: patron goddess of both kumaon and garhwal, off-limits to climbers since 1983, a unesco world heritage biosphere [v].

**the mission.** the **manaskhand mandir mala mission** — state development of kumaon's puranic temples (16, reportedly up to 48) on the char dham model; rti-confirmed at ₹113.25 crore sanctioned, ₹45.43 crore disbursed, naming jageshwar, patal bhuvaneshwar, haat kalika, bagnath, purnagiri, naina devi, kainchi dham, katarmal [v — govt rti].

| सिद्धपीठ · शक्तिपीठ | siddhapīṭh · śaktipīṭh | classes of goddess-seat (purnagiri) |
| मानसखंड | mānaskhaṇḍ | the puranic name for the kumaon sacred region |

## do this at home

- **map your family's tirths**: which of these your grandparents actually walked to — jageshwar, purnagiri, kailash — and how they traveled. pilgrimage memory is family history.
- **retell one honestly**: kasar devi's real story (vivekananda, the poets) is better than the cosmic-energy myth — tell the true one.
- **if you go**: patal bhuvaneshwar's cave and jageshwar's deodar are the two most atmospheric introductions for the young.

household plants & folk remedies जड़ी-बूटी 
> **this entry documents what kumaoni folk tradition and ethnobotanical literature record. it is not medical advice. several of these plants are toxic. nothing here is a recommendation, dosage, or endorsement of effectiveness — it is a record of belief and use.**

## in two lines

ethnobotanists have catalogued 50–56 medicinal plant species in kumaon's folk pharmacopoeia, held by village *vaidyas* whose knowledge is documented as fading with migration [v]. the everyday ones live in any hill kitchen or hedgerow — timur, kilmora, buransh, the stinging *sisūṇ* — each carrying attributed folk uses that the literature records without endorsing [v].

## what it is

**the documented pharmacopoeia.** two peer-reviewed surveys anchor this: tiwari et al. (2020) recording 56 species from 26 healers across 9 villages, and kharkwal et al. (2018) recording 50 species used by champawat vaidyas [v — t1]. the transmission of this knowledge is itself documented as disrupted by rural-urban migration [v].

**the household plants** (each use *attributed*, never endorsed): **timur** (*zanthoxylum armatum*), the "toothache tree," twigs used as chew-sticks — its sanshool compounds are pharmacologically real numbing agents [v — attributed]. **kilmora** (*berberis asiatica*), roots traditionally believed a jaundice remedy, corroborated across a tribal-ethnobotany paper, journalism, and folk sources; commercially traded [v — attributed]. **buransh** (rhododendron) flower juice, historically taken for sunstroke — with the balancing, verified fact that the plant contains natural toxins (grayanotoxin) [v — attributed + toxicity noted]. **sisūṇ** (stinging nettle, *urtica dioica* — the kumaoni name; garhwali *kandali*), food and folk remedy [v]. **jambu/faran** (*allium stracheyi*), the bhotiya spice-herb of the high valleys, attributed folk uses for colds and wounds [v].

**honesty flags:** "dolu" appears once, as a dye plant, species undetermined — no medicinal use found [u]. "sinnge" as a nettle name: not found in any source; the kumaoni term is *sisūṇ* [u — negative]. giloy sourcing is generic pan-indian, not kumaon-specific [a].

| किलमोड़ा | kilmoṛā | berberis asiatica |
| सिसूण | sisūṇ | stinging nettle (garhwali: kandali) |

## do this at home

- **learn to name them on a walk** — timur, kilmora, sisūṇ — before the naming knowledge goes; identification is the first thing lost.
- **cook the nettle**: *sisūṇ* soup is food, not medicine, and entirely safe once cooked — a living use anyone can keep.
- **record an elder's remedy** exactly as they say it, with "this is what was believed" framing — that is documentation, and it's the community phase's core.

water & sacred groves नौल-धार · देव वन 
## in two lines

a **naula** is a covered stone stair-tank built over a spring, sacred enough that a bride prays there first and a red guardian-snake is said to punish neglect; dwarahat had ~360 of them a century ago and keeps ~36 in perennial flow [v]. the groves that shelter kumaon's springs and temples — jageshwar's deodar, gangolihat's darukavan — are the same conservation instrument in tree form [v].

## what it is

**the architecture of water.** naula: a tank walled on three sides with a stepped approach on the fourth, roofed, spring-fed [v — t1]. dhara: a flowing spout, typed by posture — **sirpatia** (drink standing), **murpatia** (bending), **patveedia** (seasonal) [v — t1]. **simar/gajar**: marshy groundwater tracts turned to paddy [a — single source]. champawat's **ek hathiya naula** ("one-handed" — built overnight by a single-handed craftsman, though the legend splits: fearful "jagannath" who fled vs vengeful "rangnath" whose hand a king had cut off) and **baleshwar naula** are the famous ones; **syunrakot naula** (almora, chand-era, 14th–15th c.) became a nationally protected monument in january 2023 [v].

**the decline and the fight-back.** roughly half the himalayas' perennial springs have dried or gone seasonal (niti aayog 2018); almora town, once 300+ naulas, and dwarahat, once 360, are much diminished [v]. revival is real and named: **chirag** restored 716 springs in kumaon (2008–2019); the **naula foundation** (bishan singh baneshi, ranikhet, 2018 — founded after he lacked water for his mother's last rites) has revived 150+ [v].

**the sacred groves.** jageshwar: ~125 stone temples in a deodar forest at ~1,870 m, 7th–8th c. [v — state tourism primary]. haat kalika (gangolihat) sits in deodar the tradition calls **darukavan** [a]. a peer-reviewed western-himalaya study ties grove protection to biodiversity; named grove-deities include haat kaali, kotgyari devi, hariyali devi [v/a]. **correction:** "chiplakot" is a high alpine range and its deity, not a deodar temple-grove — retired from this category [v — negative].

| term | roman | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| नौला | naulā | covered stepped spring-tank |
| धारा | dhārā | flowing spring-spout |
| सिरपत्या · मुणपत्या · पतबिड़िया | sirpatyā · muṇpatyā · patbiṛiyā | standing / bending / seasonal dhara types |
| गूल · घराट | gūl · gharāṭ | channel · water-mill |
| दारुकावन | dārukāvan | "deodar forest" — the sacred-grove idea |

## do this at home

- **adopt your naula**: find the family spring, learn its name and type, ask who cleans it — and photograph its carved stone. neglect is the whole crisis; attention is the whole fix.
- **the bride's prayer**: if there's a wedding, keep the naula visit alive.
- **support a reviver**: chirag and the naula foundation take volunteers and donations — a diaspora contribution that lands exactly where the culture's water does.

dress & ornament पैरण-गैणा 
## in two lines

the saffron-and-red **rangwali pichhoda** — the dotted ceremonial veil every married kumaoni woman wears at weddings and pujas — is the single most distinctive garment of kumaon, gi-registered since 2023 [v — primary registry]. around it: the great gold **nath**, the crescent **bulaki**, and a wardrobe where honesty requires saying which pieces are kumaon's own and which belong to all the uttarakhand hills [v].

## what it is

**the pichhoda.** "rangwali pichhoda of kumaon" holds gi application 874, class 24, filed 19-04-2022 by a haldwani women's cooperative, **registered 08-11-2023** [v — ip india registry]. saffron/yellow ground, red dots, central swastika-sun medallion; worn by married women at weddings, janeu, naming ceremonies, festivals [a]. the folk etymology — *pichh* (back) + *orhna* (to cover) — is single-source [u].

**ornament.** the **nath**, the large gold nose-ring of the bride, hangs from a chain (*sikora*) clipped to the hair; the custom of the maternal uncle gifting it is widely repeated but not ethnographically pinned to kumaon specifically [a/u]. the crescent septum ring **bulaki** is attested for married women of both kumaon and garhwal [v]; the claim that kumaonis call it *besar* is single-source and collides with a maharashtrian jewelry term — unresolved [u]. **hansuli** (neck torque), **pahunchi** (bridal wrist), **bichhua** (toe rings), **guluband** — all real, and all explicitly documented as shared across garhwali, kumaoni, bhotiya and jaunsari women: pan-uttarakhand hill heritage, not kumaoni exclusives [v — recorded as shared].

**everyday and men's wear.** women's older daily wear is described as ghagra with a kameez/choli and orni — but this rests on thin travel-site sourcing, flagged accordingly [u]. men: dhoti/pajama-kurta, waistcoat, and cap or pagri [a — thin]. **the "pahadi topi" deserves honesty**: the round woolen cap as cold-weather wear is plausibly old, but the decorated brahmakamal cap now sold everywhere is a documented post-2022 revival product whose popularity followed a prime ministerial appearance — the archive records both halves [a — divergence between "traditional" claim and documented revival]. the bhotiya/shauka **chuba** gown is that community's own dress, recorded separately [a]; a source calling a bhotiya skirt "pichora" is a probable content-farm error, excluded [v — negative finding].

**where tradition lives now.** weddings (bride in pichhoda; groom in dhoti-kurta, choga, pagri with tikka [a — thin]), ramleela and chholiya performance — though what chholiya dancers specifically wear is an open gap. the **pankhi** shawl belongs to bhotiya weavers (panchachuli cooperative, munsiyari), not the generic kumaoni wardrobe [v — attribution corrected].

## do this at home

- **find the family pichhoda**: most kumaoni families have one folded away. photograph it, ask whose wedding it entered the family at, and note the motifs — every one is a data point.
- **ask about the nath**: whose nath does the family keep, who gifted it, at whose wedding was it last worn?
- **next wedding you attend**: notice which of these pieces appear — that's the living inventory.

everyday kumaoni dishes रोजमर्रा के कुमाऊंनी पकवान 
## in two lines

four dishes are the backbone of the everyday kumaoni kitchen: *aloo ke gutke* (dry-spiced potato), *bhatt ki churkani* (black-soybean curry made by a reverse frying technique), *dubuk* (ground soaked-dal stew), and *kaapa* (rice-flour-thickened greens). all four are cookable tonight in any city kitchen — that's the point.

## what it is

kumaoni cuisine is predominantly vegetarian, built on coarse grains (*madua* finger millet, *jhangora*), hill pulses (*bhatt* black soybean, *gahat* horse gram), and slow cooking suited to the cold himalayan environment [v: s26, s27, s30]. these four preparations plus their standard accompaniments — *bhaang* (hemp-seed) chutney and the mustard-sharp cucumber raita — form the ordinary, non-festival core of the food tradition [v].

**aloo ke gutke** — boiled potatoes cut into pieces (*gutke*), fried dry with whole red chillies, turmeric, coriander and cumin; no water at all, which is why it travels well [v: s1, s4]. a key regional marker: the kumaoni version is tempered with **jeera (cumin)**; using *jakhiya* (wild cleome viscosa seed) is the **garhwali** style [a: s1 — single explicit source; recorded prominently because blogs routinely blur this]. served with puri, madua roti, bhaang chutney, and raita [v: s1, s2, s20].

**bhatt ki churkani** — whole black soybeans fried first in ghee (the *churrr* crackle gives the dish its name [a: s7]), then sealed with wheat/rice or gram flour and slow-cooked: a reverse of the usual boil-then-temper dal method [v: s7, s11]. smoky, nutty; a winter warmer eaten with *bhaat* (rice) and coriander [v: s7, s9, s11]. kumauni.in documents three bhatt preparations: *dubuk (bhatiya)*, *churkani*, and *jauou* [a: s11].

**dubuk / dubke** — bhatt or gahat (sometimes arhar) soaked 6–8 hours, ground to a paste (*silbatta* or mixer), cooked slow with a mustard-oil tempering of cumin, garlic, hing, and green chilli, thickened with rice flour [v: s10, s11]. name likely from *dubna*, "to sink" [a: s10]. in the hills it is properly served with **jholi** (buttermilk kadhi) and bhaat [a: s12]. winter comfort food [v: s10, s12].

**kaapa** — thick green curry, most commonly spinach, thickened with roasted rice flour; hot with rice or roti; a winter dish [v: s15]. the garhwali *kafuli* is the same family of dish under a different name with minor variations — recorded as related, not identical [a: s14].

**accompaniments.** *bhaang chutney*: roasted hemp seeds ground with mint, green chilli, salt, lemon — tangy, nutty, and **not intoxicating** (hemp seed is non-psychoactive) [v: s16, s17, s23]; everyday use, with a special place at kumaoni holi [a: s17]. *kumaoni (pahadi kheera) raita*: squeezed grated cucumber in curd with coarsely ground mustard + green chilli — the mustard is what makes it kumaoni; flavours mature over 2–3 hours [a: s20]. *madua roti*: finger-millet flatbread, warming, often cut with wheat flour, the winter bread under all of the above [a: s22].

**the ingredients are the agriculture** (link: branch 4). bhatt and gahat are hardy 1500–2500 m terrace crops grown organically with minimal inputs; gahat is drought-tolerant, traditionally used against kidney stones; bhatt production has grown ~3.25% over 15 years, more stably in kumaon than garhwal [v: s24, s25, s28 — icar]. one family account records that the grandmothers' generation "had a meal of this and worked in farms and fields all day" [a: s29].

## do this at home (tonight)

**cook aloo ke gutke — 20 minutes, five ingredients.** boil 3 potatoes, cube them. heat 2 tbsp oil (mustard oil if you have it), fry 2 dry red chillies and set aside, crackle 1 tsp **cumin** (that's the kumaoni way), add ½ tsp turmeric, the potatoes, 1 tsp coriander powder, salt. toss dry on low flame till edges crisp. garnish coriander + the fried chillies [per s1, s4]. eat with curd raita. level 2: soak bhatt overnight and make churkani or dubuk on the weekend. send a photo to the family whatsapp group and ask your ija/ama how *she* makes it — her answer is community verification.

festival foods & sweets त्यारों क पकवान 
## in two lines

every kumaoni festival has an edible signature: ghughut garlands for the crows at uttarayani, ghee and stuffed bedua roti at olgia, singal spirals that a t1 field study calls "compulsory to make at every festival" [v]. and towering over them all, almora's bal mithai — chocolate-brown roasted khoya under a coat of sugar-coated poppy-seed pearls, sold from the same lal bazaar lineage since 1865 [a].

## what it is

this entry maps the sweets and festival dishes of kumaon to their occasions — because in kumaoni tradition food is not accompaniment but liturgy: the *ghughut* is an offering to crows as ancestors [a], the wedding *arsa* is a mandatory return-gift (*kaleo*) from the bride's family [a], and frying the uttarayani sweets is done in prescribed silence [a].

**bal mithai (बाल मिठाई).** roasted-khoya fudge coated in white balls — specifically **sugar-syrup-coated poppy seeds**, not plain sugar [v]. credited to halwai **joga lal sah** of lal bazaar, almora, in business from 1865, who sourced milk from **falseema village**; his shop runs in its sixth generation, and the rival house founded by his apprentices, kheem singh mohan singh rautela, still trades near the bus station [a]. almora counts 50+ bal mithai shops today [a]. **a widely repeated claim that bal mithai holds a gi tag could not be confirmed**: it is absent from the documented dec-2023 batch of 18 uttarakhand gi grants, and no registry record was found — the archive records this as *commonly claimed, unverified* [v — negative finding]. it is, verifiably, almora's product under the state's one district two products scheme [v].

**singori (सिंगौरी).** khoya, coconut and cardamom steamed in a cone of fragrant **maalu** leaf. two corrections to the popular record: the maalu is best identified as ***bauhinia vahlii*** — the great himalayan leaf-plate creeper — not *b. variegata* (kachnar) as wikipedia has it; three independent sources including the ucost director-general support *vahlii* [a — divergence recorded]. and singori is **shared heritage**: almora claims its origin, but tehri garhwal has its own thriving wedding-season singori trade — no kumaoni exclusivity is claimed here [v].

**singal (सिंगल).** fried spirals of semolina, banana, milk, curd and cardamom — per the one t1 field study of kumaoni food, "compulsory to make at every festival," and notoriously hard to shape well [v — t1]. distinct from nepali *sel roti* (rice flour) despite casual conflation [a].

**ghughut (घुघुत).** for ghughutia/uttarayani (14 jan 2027): flour kneaded in jaggery syrup, shaped like the numeral ४ — plus *damru*, *talwar*, *dhaal*, *tokri* shapes — sun-dried, then fried after dark in strict silence ("talk, and the ghughute will fly away," children are told) [a]. strung with peanuts and fruit into garlands children wear at dawn while singing the crow songs — **काले कावा काले, घुघुती माला खा ले** — and no one eats before the crows, who stand for the ancestors [a]. district rhythm differs: almora, champawat, nainital and u.s. nagar make them on magh 1 and feed crows on magh 2; across the ramganga, pithoragarh and bageshwar make them the evening before [a — named-district detail].

**the rest of the map.** harela: kheer, puri, urad bade, pua [v]. ghee sankranti: bedua roti, lagad, gabe (see its own entry). weddings: arsa in the *kaleo* [a] — though arsa itself is contested kumaon/garhwal heritage, recorded as shared [v]. diwali: singal, khajur (the fried fennel-jaggery cookie, **not** the date fruit) [a], and khil-daadim-walnut rounds of the house in a soop [u]. *jhangora ki kheer*, widely marketed as kumaoni, is per the t1 study a **garhwali import** — the grain's kumaoni name is *maadira* [a — divergence recorded].

## do this at home

- **order the real thing**: bal mithai ships from almora shops — ask relatives for the shop their family swears by, and note the answer (shop loyalty is oral history).
- **cook the gateway sweet**: singal is the festival skill worth learning — semolina, ripe banana, curd, cardamom; pipe spirals into hot ghee. expect to fail twice; that's traditional.
- **mark the calendar**: 14 january 2027 — make ghughut with children, string the mala, learn one line of काले कावा काले, and let the crows eat first.
- **ask one elder** which sweet their village wedding *kaleo* had to include — arsa, rot, or something this entry doesn't know yet.

the kumaoni kitchen: hearth, vessels, table रस्या-भानि 
## in two lines

the traditional rasyā runs on a mud **chulha** re-plastered daily, almora copper (**gagar**, **parat**, worship vessels — the tamta trade that named a mohalla), the bell-metal **bhaddu** that slow-cooks the bhatt, and stone: **silbatta** for chutneys, **okhli-musal** for dhan [v/a]. feasts are eaten off **maalu** and **timil** leaf-plates; grain winters in the deodar **bhakar** [v/a].

## what it is

**the hearth.** mud chulha, floor-level, re-plastered with mud/gobar daily — documented in ethnographic photo-reportage of a living kumaoni kitchen [v — t2 field essay]. **honesty first:** the purity rules this project expected to find — entry restrictions, bathing-before-cooking, a first-offering to the fire, gendered serving order — have **no kumaon-specific documentation online**; they are held as elder questions, not published as fact [u — negatives recorded].

**the vessels.** **bhaddu** — bell-metal/panchdhatu pot for slow dals, confirmed independently by a dictionary source and food journalism [v]; **tamra gagri/gagar** — the copper water vessel, a documented dowry item [a]; **parat**, **tola**, ritual vessels from almora's tamta lanes ("tamar nagari") [a]; iron kadhai for palak-kaapa class dishes [a — thin]; **silbatta** and **okhli/ukhal-musal** everywhere [v]. "phukni" (blow-pipe) as a kumaoni term: unverified [u].

**the table.** feast food served on **maalu** (*bauhinia vahlii*) and **timil** (*ficus auriculata*) leaf-plates and dona bowls — both botanically confirmed [v]; floor seating in rows (pangat-style) attested generally, its kumaoni etiquette detail thin [a/u]. **the store.** wooden **bhakar/kothar** granaries — deodar-built, some with chain-and-bell rigging against theft; construction detail leans garhwal-documented, extrapolation flagged [a — boundary noted]. sun-dried badi-mangodi preservation documented in the everyday-dishes entry [—].

| रस्या | rasyā | the kitchen |
| भड्डू | bhaḍḍū | bell-metal slow-cooking pot |

| सिलबट्टा · ओखली-मूसल | silbaṭṭā · okhlī-mūsal | grinding stone · mortar-pestle |
| मालू · तिमिल | mālū · timil | the two leaf-plate leaves |
| भकार | bhakār | the grain store |

## do this at home

- **inventory the copper**: most kumaoni families own one gagar or parat — photograph it, ask which wedding it entered at, and whether it came from almora.
- **one silbatta chutney** (bhang or pudina) ground by hand — texture is the lesson.
- **ask the purity questions** this entry couldn't answer: who could enter the rasyā? was there a fire-offering? their answers close four open gaps.

aipan — ritual art of thresholds ऐपण 
## in two lines

aipan is kumaon's own geometry of welcome: rice-paste white dripped by a woman's ring finger over red geru earth, at thresholds, around pujas, under wedding feet — and a design without its dots is not merely unfinished, it means mourning [v]. once fading, it now holds a national gi registration ("uttarakhand aipan," certified 2021) and two women-led revivals that turned grandmothers' floors into working income [v].

## what it is

aipan (ऐपण) is the ritual floor-and-wall art of kumaon, drawn by women and passed mother to daughter [v]. the ground is **geru** (गेरू) — red-ochre clay; the line is **biswar** (बिस्वार) — rice soaked overnight and ground to a free-flowing paste [v — two independent t1 sources]. the precise work — the dripped lines of the *vasudhara*, the ceremonial central motif — is done with the **ring finger** (*anamika*) [v]; broader fills use the last three fingers of the right hand [a]. the red field is read as fortune and fertility [a]; even the etymology is contested between sanskrit *lepana* ("to plaster") and *arpan* ("offering") — both academic-adjacent, recorded unresolved [a].

every design begins and ends with the **bindu**, the dot of creation [v]. the grammar has a hard rule: **a design must end in dots** — dotless aipan is drawn only on the 12th day after a death, then rubbed out with mud and redrawn with dots when mourning ends [v — two independent institutional sources]. aipan is specifically kumaoni within a wider indian family — bengal's *alpona*, bihar's *aripana*, rajasthan's *mandana* are cousins, not the same practice [v]; its presence in garhwali homes today is described as modern spread from kumaon, not a parallel tradition [a]. the popular chand-dynasty origin story is repeated everywhere but never with a primary citation — recorded as tradition, not history [u].

**recognition and revival.** the gi record is primary-verified: **"uttarakhand aipan," application 648, filed 27-05-2019, registered 14-09-2021**, applicant a women's cooperative (ulka nidhi swayat sahkarita) of **pithoragarh**, with 180+ named women artisans across five kumaon districts as authorized users [v — ip india registry]. two named revivals anchor the present: **namita tiwari's "cheli aipan"** (almora, 2015) — triple-corroborated, including her name on the gi users list [v] — and **minakshi khati's "minakriti — the aipan project"** (chhoi village, ramnagar, dec 2019), which by 2021 reporting employed ~30 women and had taught thousands of children [a]. a related but distinct gi covers the **rangwali pichhoda of kumaon** (2023) — the aipan-patterned ceremonial shawl, not aipan itself [v].

## how it is practised

the motif vocabulary is occasion-locked — each ceremony has its chowki (seat-diagram):

| motif | occasion |
|---|---|
| **वसुधारा** — dripped vertical lines, always odd-numbered (5/7/9/11) | thresholds, puja places, the tulsi; "without vasudhara the aipan is incomplete" [v] |
| **सरस्वती चौकी** | a child's first day of study [a] |
| **नामकरण / सूर्य दर्शन चौकी** | naming ceremony, 11th day — baby's first sun [a] |
| **जनेऊ चौकी** — sapt-rishi stars in a hexagon | sacred-thread ceremony [a] |
| **धुलिअर्घ्य · आचार्य चौकी** | wedding — groom's feet-washing; the priest's seat [a] |
| **लक्ष्मी यंत्र · लक्ष्मी पग** — footprints entrance→puja room | diwali [v] |
| **नवदुर्गा चौकी** | navratri [a] |
| **शिव/शिवार्चन पीठ** | shiva worship, sawan mondays [a] |
| **ज्योति पट्ट (ज्यूंती)** — the three jeev matrikas | weddings & janeu; linked by tradition to a 9th-c. matrika panel at bageshwar [a] |
| **दुर्गा थापा** | the two navratras — paper painting, the fullest composition [a] |

traditionally the floor was first cleaned with cow-dung wash, then geru, then biswar — cemented floors have thinned the first step [a]. daily practice put fresh aipan on the threshold each morning [a]; today the full art concentrates on festivals, weddings, and commissions — canvas, pichhoda, nameplates — the commercial adaptation the revivals run on [v].

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| ऐपण | aipaṇ | the art itself |
| गेरू | gerū | red-ochre clay ground |
| बिस्वार | bisvār | rice-paste white |
| वसुधारा | vasudhārā | the odd-numbered dripped threshold lines |
| चौकी | chaukī | occasion-specific seat-diagram |
| बिंदु | bindu | the dot — start, end, and completeness |
| ज्यूंती | jyūṁtī | the matrika wedding/janeu painting |
| अनामिका | anāmikā | the ring finger — the instrument |

## do this at home

- **draw a vasudhara this week** (paper version): paint a palm-sized patch terracotta-red; mix rice flour with water to a thin cream; with your ring finger, drip **five** vertical lines; finish with a row of dots. you have made the smallest complete aipan.
- **rule to teach children**: count the lines — always odd; end with dots — always.
- **look at the real thing**: the archive's own site mark and borders are aipan-derived; compare them with a family photo of a grandmother's threshold if one exists — and send us that photo (no licensed aipan photograph exists anywhere on wikimedia commons; family photos are the fix).
- **diwali 2026**: draw lakshmi's footprints from the front door inward before the puja.

## variations & open questions

etymology (lepana vs arpan) unresolved [a/a]. chand-era origin: tradition, uncited [u]. the kanyadaan chowki (19-dot bhadra) and the sah-community *nata* wall painting rest on one commercial source [u]. a "lakshmi narayan chowki" named in early project notes was **not found** in any source — premise corrected, not published. whether harela's *dikare* are properly "decorated with aipan" needs a stronger citation [u]. finger-technique variation across sources likely reflects different sub-techniques (dripping vs filling), specified per claim here [a].

chholiya, ramleela & folk theatre छोलिया 
## in two lines

**chholiya** is the sword-and-shield wedding dance of kumaon — white churidar, sandal-paste faces, tulwar and brass shield, said to descend from marriage-by-swordpoint — danced ahead of every traditional baraat [v]. almora's **sung ramleela** (1860, staged in classical ragas) is genuinely named in unesco's 2008 india-wide ramlila inscription — as one of seven exemplars, not with its own listing; the "world's longest-running opera per unesco" line is unsupported and retired [v — corrected].

## what it is

**chholiya.** occasions: weddings/baraat foremost, now also festivals and state events (~3,000 artists for a 2023 pm visit, a world book of records entry) [v]. the ensemble is a social cross-section documented by field journalism: rajput sword-dancers, dalit dholi drummers, turi/ransingha with bairagi-jogi-gosain players [v — pari]. **the masakbeen, resolved:** the bagpipe in chholiya ensembles is a **scottish highland bagpipe — a post-1814 colonial regimental import**, with no evidence of pre-british existence (peer-reviewed ethnomusicology, alter), since assimilated so thoroughly it reads as folk [v — closes this archive's oldest open gap]. costume and symbolism per the aggregator record; groom's title "kunwar" ties the war-dance frame to the wedding [a].

**ramleela & the rest.** almora's ramleela: from 1860 (badridat joshi, badreshwar maidan), sung in ragas — malkauns to jhinjhoti [v — two outlets]; the hukka club has staged it since 1930 [a]. unesco 2008 inscription no. 00110 names almora among india's "most representative" ramlilas [v — primary]. **swang** — the satirical impersonation theatre — lives inside mahila holi (see the holi entry); distinct from haryana's saang [a].

- **at the next wedding with chholiya**, name the parts for the children — dancers, dholi, the bagpipe's odd history — the ensemble is a social history lesson in motion.
- **watch almora's ramleela** if there in dussehra season: sung theatre of this kind survives almost nowhere else.
- **retell the masakbeen story**: a scottish pipe that became kumaoni — the archive's best parable that living culture absorbs and owns.

folk music & its instruments लोक संगीत 
## in two lines

kumaon's soundscape rests on the **hurka** — the hourglass drum without which no jagar can be held — carried by hereditary hurkiya musicians of historically marginalized communities whose repertoire *is* the region's oral literature [v]. around it: the shared pan-uttarakhand **dhol-damau**, the battle-brass **turi** and **ransingha**, the colonial-import-turned-native **masakbeen**, and the song "bedu pako baramasa," which mohan and naima upreti carried from almora to the nation [v/a].

## what it is

**the instruments.** hurka: wood body, goat-skin heads, the jagar's engine [v]. **dhol-damau: shared across garhwal, kumaon and himachal — not kumaon-exclusive**, with the dhol sagar treatise tied academically to garhwali drumming families [v — boundary recorded]. turi, nagphani, ransingha: the brass battle-horns of processions [a]. masakbeen: colonial scottish bagpipe, assimilated (see chholiya entry) [v]. binai (jaw harp): near-vanished, single-source [u].

**the musicians, documented respectfully.** the hereditary players — **hurkiya, bajgi, das, dholi, auji** — belong to historically "low-caste," economically marginal communities; academic sources document both their centrality (guardians of the epics, first invocation rights at sowing) and the modern attrition as stigma and dj-band economics push the young out [v — t1 journals + sahapedia]. this archive records their names as artists first.

**the carriers to the nation.** **mohan upreti** (1928–97): composer-director, "bedu pako baramasa," founder of parvatiya kala kendra (1968), documenter of malushahi [v]. **naima khan upreti** (1938–2018): sang the 1952 premiere and 1955 hmv recording of bedu pako; nsd-trained, doordarshan producer, pkk's later president [v]. **girish tiwari "girda"** (1945–2010): poet-singer of the movements — chipko to statehood [a]. air almora and the 1955–78 air field-recording program shaped and preserved the canon [a/v].

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| हुड़का | huṛkā | the hourglass drum |
| हुड़किया · बाजगी · औजी | huṛkiyā · bājgī · aujī | the hereditary musician communities |
| ढोल-दमाऊँ | ḍhol-damāūñ | the paired drums (pan-uttarakhand) |
| तुरी · रणसिंघा | turī · raṇsinghā | the brass horns |
| बेडु पाको बारामासा | beḍu pāko bārāmāsā | the unofficial anthem |

## do this at home

- **learn bedu pako** — one verse, everyone can; it is the lowest-friction piece of kumaoni identity in existence (and the kumaon regiment marches to it).
- **at any jagar or wedding**, find the hurka player, learn his name, and thank him by it — the archive's respect principle in one gesture.
- **play the instruments game** with children at a baraat: dhol or damau? turi or masakbeen?

likhai: the carved house लिखाई 
## in two lines

likhai — "writing" — is the woodcarving that turned kumaoni facades into pages: ganesha over nearly every carved **kholi** doorway, vines of lakshmi's lotus down the jambs, named window-forms (**tyapari**, two- and three-panelled) [v — incl. peer-reviewed fieldwork]. its master generation is gone — the hindu profiled ganga ram as "the last surviving master craftsman" before his death in 2018 — but munsiari's ordh carvers and an nmhs-funded training revival keep the chisel moving [v/a].

## what it is

**the grammar.** kholi = the carved ceremonial doorway (the term one institutional source labels garhwali but every source uses; **"daroj" appears in no source beyond the one academic architecture paper** — the naming conflict now leans kholi, recorded) [a — updated]. ganesha top-center; lotus/vine = lakshmi; geometric panels a later layer; munsiari adds tibetan-influenced dragons and arch-forms from the border trade [v — fieldwork]. windows: **tyapari**, dodyari/teen-dyari [a].

**the carvers.** caste attribution genuinely conflicts: tourism sources say "oar"/shilpkar; academic fieldwork (20-village munsiari survey) names the historical carving caste as dom, later folded into the shilpkar category — both recorded, likely historical-name vs modern-category [a — conflict held]. spread: all six hill districts of kumaon, while garhwal's carving concentrates in temples rather than houses [a].

**decline & revival.** the causal chain is documented: colonial timber extraction → forest-law limits → timber costs → brick/rcc — down to owners molding cement to imitate carving [v — fieldwork]. almora's bazaar lanes keep facades above the shopfronts [a]. revival: national mission on himalayan studies-funded training via himal prakriti (munsiari) [a]; a national handicrafts-catalogue listing exists, but **gi status is not confirmed against the registry** — recorded as unverified, unlike aipan/ringal/tamta whose registrations are primary-verified [a/u — discipline kept].

- **photograph the kholi**: if the ancestral house stands, its doorway is the family's most endangered artwork — shoot it straight-on and in raking light, note the motifs, send it in.
- **read one facade** in almora's bazaar: find the ganesha, then the vine — once seen, never unseen.
- **ask who carved it**: family memory of the carpenter's name/community is exactly what the caste-attribution conflict needs.

ringal basketry & tamta copperwork रिंगाल · टम्टा 
## in two lines

two gi-registered crafts hold up the kumaoni household: **ringal** dwarf-bamboo weaving (the doka on every back, the soop in every courtyard) and **tamta** copperwork, the almora trade that named a whole mohalla "tamar nagari" [v — both on the ip india register: "uttarakhand ringal craft" app. 652, "uttarakhand tamta product" app. 653]. both are down to their last practitioners — ~5 active tamta families where ~72 worked a generation ago [v].

## what it is

**ringal.** products: doka (carrying basket), soop/suppa (winnowing), mats and dolls; clusters across bageshwar, pithoragarh, almora (and garhwal districts — a shared hill craft) [a/v]. field reporting names the **bajela** community of pithoragarh among hereditary weavers — while this project's earlier "barudi/rudia" and "dwali village" notes were **found in no source and are retired** [v — premise corrected]. no state minimum price; decline documented [v — pari].

**tamta.** almora's coppersmiths — gagar, parat, tola, ritual vessels — with the chand-era origin (craftsmen brought when the capital moved, 1560s) held as **legend on top of documented history** (the capital shift itself is fact) [a — labeled]. named living master: naveen chandra "nabboo" tamta, state "shilpratan" holder [v]. the colonial copper-mining-restriction decline story appears in two near-identical tellings — counted as one chain [a].

**the gi record, corrected.** the state-wise ip india register lists both crafts against **27.05.2019** — the same date as aipan's *filing*; since aipan's detail page shows filing 2019/certification 2021, the "registered 2021" vs "2019" divergence is a **filing-vs-certification column ambiguity**, recorded here rather than silently picked; earlier "gi 2021" phrasing in this archive's hill-life entry is now flagged to this note [a — ambiguity held; detail-page fetch queued].

education in kumaon पढ़ै-लिखै 
## in two lines

education is kumaon's oldest export and its newest wound: a region famous for its pandit-scholar lineages and colonial-era schools (ramsay 1871, sherwood 1869) that today watches village schools close behind the families leaving — 21 shut in pithoragarh in a single recent year [v/a]. the story runs from almora's mission classrooms to asia-notable institutions: india's first agricultural university at pantnagar, the aries observatory above nainital [v].

## what it is

**the old reputation.** kumaon's brahmin lineages (pant, joshi, pande) carry a scholar-administrator tradition — though the popular maharashtrian-migration origin story rests on community histories, not yet academic sourcing [a/u]. almora's intellectual aura is well-documented: vivekananda's repeated stays (1890, 1897–98) [a], uday shankar's dance academy (1938–44) drawing zohra sehgal and guru dutt [v], and boshi sen's vivekananda laboratory (1936), whose agricultural research fed the green revolution and lives on as icar-vpkas [v — institutional primary].

**the colonial classrooms.** a mission school in almora (1844 or 1850 — sources conflict, recorded) became **ramsay college in 1871**, alma mater of govind ballabh pant [a/v]. nainital stacked up boarding schools: **sherwood college (1869)** — corbett's school — **st. joseph's (1888)**, and post-independence **birla vidya mandir (1947)**, founded at pant's urging [v].

**higher education today.** **kumaun university (1973)** — dsb nainital plus bhimtal, with almora's colleges spun out in 2020 into **soban singh jeena university** [v]. **g.b. pant university, pantnagar (1960)** — independent india's first agricultural university, inaugurated by nehru, in kumaon's tarai [v]. **government medical college, haldwani — founded 1997** (not 2001, a correction this research produced), first mbbs batch 2004 [v]. **uttarakhand open university, haldwani (2005)** [v]. nit uttarakhand sits in garhwal — noted to keep the map honest [v]. research anchors: **aries** (the 1954 up state observatory, at nainital's manora peak since 1955/61) and **g.b. pant national institute of himalayan environment** (kosi-katarmal, almora, 1988–89) [v — institutional primaries].

**the present, honestly.** 2011 census literacy: almora 80.5%, nainital 83.9%, pithoragarh 82.3% — with rural female literacy trailing male by ~20+ points everywhere (figures from census aggregators, primary-pdf verification queued) [a]. the palayan link is direct: ~16–17% of out-migrants cite absent schools as a reason for leaving [v — commission-linked reporting]; recent reporting counts 21 schools closed in pithoragarh in 2023–24 and, statewide, most schools without a principal [a — primary report not yet located]. the result is the classic kumaoni arc — educated in the hills, employed in the plains — the **"money-order economy"** of army, administration and teaching remittances [a — term's coinage untraced].

| पाठशाला | pāṭhshālā | the traditional village school |

| पलायन | palāyan | the out-migration that empties the schools |

## do this at home

- **trace one school**: ask parents and grandparents which school each generation attended, and where it was — three generations usually spans a village pathshala to a metro college; that arc *is* the education story of kumaon.
- **if the village school still runs**, ask how many children attend now versus in your parent's time. send us both numbers.
- **visit one anchor**: aries' public outreach nights, or the pant museum in almora.

hill life & the kumaoni home पहाड़ौ जीवन 
## in two lines

the traditional kumaoni house is a machine for mountain winters: cattle in the ground-floor **goth** warming the living floor above, slate **patal** roof pitched low to hold heat, carved wooden facade announcing the family's standing [v]. around it runs the village's real infrastructure — the sacred **naula** water spring, the terraced fields, and labor shared between households [v].

## what it is

**the house.** academic fieldwork records the grammar precisely: ground floor **goth** for cattle and grain; upstairs a living room (**chakh**), bedroom (**bhiter**), kitchen (**rashya**); windows (**chajj**); the main door — called **daroj** in the one kumaon-specific academic study, though tourism writing uses **kholi**, a term one institutional source labels garhwali — divergence recorded, unresolved [v/a]. the roof is flat slate (**patal/pather**) at a shallow 15–17° pitch, engineered to keep heat in [v — academic]. the carved facades are **likhai**, walnut-wood carving by master craftsmen of the shilpkar community — a documented dying craft [v]. related families build in rows: the **bakhali**, attested back to atkinson's 1880s gazetteer [v].

**water.** the **naula** — a stepped, temple-roofed spring chamber, often crowned with a stone read as vishnu — is kumaon's signature water architecture, and it is ritual infrastructure: a new bride's first act at her husband's village is prayer at the naula or dhara [v — peer-reviewed, csir journal]. communal **panghat** rules (cleanliness, no animals) were once panchayat duties by royal decree [v].

**the seasons.** terrace agriculture runs a three-season calendar (kharif/chaumas, rabi/hyund, jayad), historically with **barahnaja**-style mixed sowing [v — peer-reviewed]. the costliest documented fact of hill life: women carry the disproportionate load — fodder, fuel, water, up to twice-daily forest trips — a burden extensively studied in the academic literature [v]. collective field labor with **hudkiya bol** work-songs (a hurka drummer singing the village through paddy transplanting) is the attested kumaoni form of shared work [a]; a specific term like "palta/parma" for labor exchange could not be verified [u — recorded honestly].

**the objects.** **ringal** (dwarf bamboo) baskets — the back-carried **doka** among them — hold a gi tag (2021) [v]; **tamta** copperwork of almora traces, by legend, to coppersmiths the chand kings brought when the capital moved to almora in the 1560s [a — legend, labeled]. 

**the change.** the 2011 census counted 1,048 zero-population villages in uttarakhand; the state migration commission now counts ~1,787 fully abandoned [v]. the bhotiya/shauka communities of the high pithoragarh valleys — whose trans-himalayan trade and transhumance ended with the 1962 border closure — are a distinct story, documented here as their own, not folded into "kumaoni lifestyle" generally [v — flagged community-specific].

- **ask for the house tour**: have an elder sketch their childhood home — which room was the goth, where the chakh was. label it in kumaoni. that sketch is family archive material.
- **find your naula**: on the next village visit, ask to be shown the naula your family drank from, and who maintains it now.
- **one object**: if the family owns a doka, a copper vessel, or a likhai fragment — photograph it, note who made it, send it in.
